fact: May 21 is exactly 31 days before the summer solstice in the Northern Hemisphere - that's like counting down to the longest day of the year!
In Alaska, the sun stays up for almost 19 hours on this date, which means kids there get to play outside until nearly midnight!
Cicadas often emerge from underground around this time after spending 13 or 17 years buried in dirt - imagine sleeping that long!
This date falls in the middle of Gemini season, when the zodiac sign is associated with being chatty and curious.
Dandelions are typically at their fluffiest around May 21 in many temperate regions - those white puffballs you blow to make wishes are actually tiny parachutes for seeds!
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, May 21 marks the heart of autumn when leaves turn brilliant orange and red.
Fireflies usually start blinking around this time in North America - they're basically flying disco lights looking for mates!
May 21 is the 141st day of the year, which means there are exactly 224 days left until New Year's Eve.
Morning dew gets especially heavy around this date in temperate climates because warm days and cool nights create the perfect recipe for water droplets.
Strawberry plants in many northern regions are busy making their juiciest berries right around May 21 - the fruits are swelling up like tiny red balloons!
Baby birds are often learning to fly around this time as parents teach them to leave the nest - it's like nature's version of flight school!
The moon can appear extra orange or red when it rises around this date because spring air holds more moisture and pollen that filters the light.
